message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To describe physical behaviours following hospital admission for COVID-19 including associations with acute illness severity and ongoing symptoms.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>1077 patients with COVID-19 discharged from hospital between March and November 2020 were recruited. Using a 14-day wear protocol, wrist-worn accelerometers were sent to participants after a five-month follow-up assessment. Acute illness severity was assessed by the WHO clinical progression scale, and the severity of ongoing symptoms was assessed using four previously reported data-driven clinical recovery clusters. Two existing control populations of office workers and type 2 diabetes were comparators.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Valid accelerometer data from 253 women and 462 men were included. Women engaged in a mean±SD of 14.9±14.7 minutes/day of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA), with 725.6±104.9 minutes/day spent inactive and 7.22±1.08 hours/day asleep. The values for men were 21.0±22.3 and 755.5±102.8 minutes/day and 6.94±1.14 hours/day, respectively. Over 60% of women and men did not have any days containing a 30-minute bout of MVPA. Variability in sleep timing was approximately 2 hours in men and women. More severe acute illness was associated with lower total activity and MVPA in recovery. The very severe recovery cluster was associated with fewer days/week containing continuous bouts of MVPA, longer sleep duration, and higher variability in sleep timing. Patients post-hospitalisation with COVID-19 had lower levels of physical activity, greater sleep variability, and lower sleep efficiency than a similarly aged cohort of office workers or those with type 2 diabetes.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Physical activity and regulating sleep patterns are potential treatable traits for COVID-19 recovery programmes.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Device-assessed sleep and physical activity in individuals recovering from a hospital admission for COVID-19: a prospective, multicentre study

reference.unstructured: World Health Organisation. WHO Coronavirus (COVID-19) Dashboard. https://covid19.who.int/ (accessed 21/01/2022).
Public Health England. Coronavirus (COVID-19) in the UK. https://coronavirus.data.gov.uk/ (accessed 21/01/2022).
